summary:
Cyprus, an island in the eastern Mediterranean some 65–75 kilometers offshore from the nearest Levantine coast, was colonized by early Neolithic communities from the mainland during the Pre-Pottery Neolithic period — among the earliest known intentional maritime colonization of an uninhabited island in human history. The colonization of Cyprus is dated to approximately 9800–8800 BCE based on radiocarbon dates from the earliest known Cypriot sites, including Akrotiri Aetokremnos and Shillourokambos.

The colonization of Cyprus required deliberate seaborne crossings of open water, implying the availability of watercraft capable of carrying people and, crucially, the livestock and seed stock needed to establish a self-sustaining farming community on the island. Animals including cattle, sheep, goats, pigs, deer, and [[event:1398|cats]] were all introduced to Cyprus from the mainland, none being native to the island — this was a planned colonization, not an accidental landfall. Cyprus is thus one of the earliest examples of deliberate long-distance maritime colonization in prehistory.

The earliest Cypriot settlers appear to have been PPNA or early PPNB communities from the coastal Levant. They found an island with no indigenous mammalian predators, where deer and pygmy hippopotami (both now extinct) could be hunted with little difficulty. The colonization of Cyprus demonstrates that early Neolithic communities possessed the maritime skills, watercraft, and organizational capacity to undertake intentional seaborne settlement of offshore islands — a capability that, developed and refined over subsequent millennia, would eventually enable the colonization of remote islands across the Mediterranean and ultimately the Pacific Ocean.
